section: CHARACTERISTICS OF $\mathsf{NaBF}_4$ NaF (92-8 mole %) IN HASTELLOY N
text: CHARACTERISTICS OF $\mathsf{NaBF}_4$ NaF (92-8 mole %) IN HASTELLOY N
CORROSION AND MASS TRANSFER CHARACTERISTICS OF $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ -NaF (92-8 mole %)

A series of corrosion experiments in thermal convection and pump loops designed to test the compatibility of fused $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ -8 mole % NaF with Hastelloy N has shown the extreme effect of impurities on the system mass transfer.
Titanium-modified Hastelloy N with less chromium and iron shows a greater resistance to attack in a fluoroborate salt with 500 ppm oxide impurity than does standard Hastelloy N.
Its corrosion rate is doubled by increasing the oxide concentration to 1500 ppm.

It now appears that the best choice for the MSBR secondary coolant is the eutectic mixture of 8 mole % NaF in $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ , with a melting point<sup>1</sup> of $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $725^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) as shown in Fig.

At elevated temperatures the fluoroborates show an appreciable equilibrium pressure of gaseous BF₃; however, at the proposed maximum temperature of the MSBR secondary coolant (621°C) the pressure is only 252 torr.
The equilibrium pressure² above a melt of NaBF₄-8 mole % NaF is given as a function of temperature by

Also $\mathrm{BF}_3$ and glass reacted at an appreciable rate just above $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Under the conditions of those experiments, $\mathrm{BF}_3$ did not appreciably attack a wide variety of metals or alloys examined at temperatures up to $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

All metal components of the MSRE in contact with molten salt were made of Hastelloy $^{7}$ N (formerly called INOR-8).

Two decades of corrosion testing $^{8-15}$ and experience $^{16-19}$ with the MSRE have demonstrated the excellent compatibility of Hastelloy N and graphite with fluoride salts containing LiF, $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , $\mathrm{UF}_4$ , and $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ .
7Hastelloy N is the trade name of Cabot Corporation for a nickel-base alloy containing $16\%$ Mo, $7\%$ Cr, $5\%$ Fe, and $0.057\%$ C.

Very pure (> 99.9%) starting materials were evacuated to about 380 torr, heated to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a vessel lined with nickel, and then held for about 15 hr under these conditions.

If the rise in pressure was not excessive (indicating no volatile impurities), the salt was heated to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ while still under vacuum and agitated for a few hours with bubbling helium.

The hot portion of each thermal convection loop was heated by sets of clamshell heaters, with the input power controlled by silicon controlled rectifiers (SCR units) and the temperature controlled by a Leeds and Northrup Speedomax H series 60 type CAT controller.
Each loop was degreased with ethyl alcohol, heated to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under vacuum to remove moisture, and leak checked before filling with salt.
Each loop was filled by heating it, the salt pot, and all connecting lines to at least $530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and applying helium pressure to the salt pot to force the salt into the loop.
The first charge of salt was circulated under a small temperature difference $(20^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ for 24 hr and dumped.
A helium cover gas under slight positive pressure (about 5 psig) was maintained over the salt in the loops during operation.

The results of tests of the fluoroborate salt mixture in two thermal convection loops and in a series of capsule tests have already been reported.[20,21] In addition, four other thermal convection loops and one pumped loop have been operated.

Loop NCL-17, constructed of commercial Hastelloy N with removable specimens in each leg, is being operated to determine the effect of steam inleakage in a fluoroborate salt-Hastelloy N circuit.
The maximum temperature is $607^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a temperature difference of $107^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
This experiment has run for over 10,000 hr and is continuing in order to provide information on the immediate and long-range corrosion of the system after steam injection.
The loop was operated for 1050 hr, the specimens were removed and weighed, and steam was forced into the flowing salt system through a
16-mil hole in a closed 1/4-in.
Figure 10 shows the weight changes and temperatures for the specimens in NCL-17.
Figure 11 shows the specimens from the loop 424 hr after exposure to steam.
For the last 3500 hr of operation, as shown in Table 4, the concentrations of impurity constituents in the salt (Fe, Cr, Ni, Mo, and O) remained relatively constant.
The present chromium level is about 320 ppm.

In loop NCL-20, constructed of standard Hastelloy N with removable specimens in each leg, the fluoroborate coolant salt has circulated for 12,000 hr.
The salt used was especially pure (see Table 6).

The loop is being operated at temperature conditions very near those proposed for the maximum $(687^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and minimum $(387^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ salt-metal temperature (primary heat exchanger and steam generator, respectively) of the MSBR secondary circuit.

Forced air cooling (as opposed to ambient air cooling used on other thermal-convection loops) is used on the lower half of the cold leg, and the practical operating temperatures obtained were $687^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ maximum and about $437^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ minimum: a temperature difference of $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Table 6 gives the corrosion results for this experiment.
Figure 14 shows weight changes of the specimens as functions of time and temperature.

The MSR-FCL-1 forced-circulation loop operated for over 11,000 hr (7 runs) to test the compatibility of standard Hastelloy N with $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole % NaF coolant salt at temperatures and flow rates similar to those that existed in the MSRE coolant circuit.
Salt velocity in the 1/2-in.-OD $\times 0.042$ -in.-wall Hastelloy N loop was nominally 10 fps and was limited by the use of an available pump.

Figure 16 is a typical temperature profile of the system; this profile was based on a computer plot of the temperature information from the test loop and on heat transfer calculations that estimate the inner wall temperatures.
The maximum and minimum salt temperatures of the loop are 588 and $510^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Hastelloy N corrosion test specimens were exposed to the circulating salt at three temperatures, 510, 555, and $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
After 659 hr of shakedown operation under various conditions and the initial 2082 hr of operation at design conditions a bearing failed in the LFB centrifugal pump.
This first operating period was designated run 1.
It was then decided that the loop would be shut down each 2000 hr for pump-bearing replacement and specimen examination.
The operating times and corrosion rates for the first five runs are summarized in Table 7, and Fig.
18 shows the weight changes of specimens as measured after each run.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: In contrast to the deposits of material containing $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{CrF}_{6}$ , which had been found $^{26}$ on a cold finger in PKP-1 loop at metal wall temperatures of 400, 460, and $477^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , no significant deposit of any kind was seen on the cold-finger tests in loop MSR-FCL-1.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Even in the three tests where indicated wall temperatures were below the salt liquidus temperature $(385^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , the surface of the cold finger was essentially clean, as visually observed when it was withdrawn into a sight glass.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Occasionally, small patches (1/8 to 1/4 in. across) of white material, estimated to be a few mils thick, were seen on the surface.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: The cold finger was then installed in the PKP-1 fluoroborate loop in an attempt to duplicate the previous cold-finger test results of that loop.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: The chromium concentration of the fluoroborate salt in loop PKP-1 is about 500 ppm, while that in MSR-FCL-1 is about 250 ppm.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Two tests were run in which the indicated cold-finger wall temperature was about $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (salt temperature in the pump bowl was $548^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Test times were 1 and 4.5 hr.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Since the cold finger previously used in the PKP-1 loop contained grooves on the outside surface, the lower half of the surface of the MSR-FCL-1 cold finger was scored with file marks about 0.010 in. deep, and a third test run at about $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lasting 6 1/2 hr was made in the PKP-1 loop.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: The total deposit weight was 1.63 g, of which 1.26 g was mostly green material (complete separation was not possible).

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Chemical analysis of the green material disclosed 2.93 wt % Cr, 1.03 wt % Fe, 370 ppm Ni, and < 500 ppm Mo, with the remainder Na, B, and F.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Stoichiometric calculations indicated 11 mole % Na $_3$ CrF $_6$ , 4 mole % Na $_3$ FeF $_6$ , and the remainder a mixture of NaBF $_4$ and NaF.

section: Installation and Testing of Cold Finger During Run 3
text: Subsequent operation of the cold finger at the same conditions in loop MSR-FCL-1 did not produce a deposit.
After run 4 when the pump had been removed, we found approximately $50\mathrm{g}$ of intermixed white and green material on the pump liner above the liquid level.

Analysis of a small portion of this material showed about equal amounts of NaF and $\mathrm{NaBF_4}$ , with $4300~\mathrm{ppm}$ Cr, which we assume to be in the form of $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{CrF}_6$ .
Specimens in the coldest part of the system gained weight during the first three runs and then lost a small amount of weight during run 4.
Specimens at other locations in the loop (555 or $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) showed similar but smaller thickness variations.
Figure 27 shows a specimen at $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after run 4 and clearly illustrates the heavier attack at the upstream end.
Changes in specimen thickness are summarized in Table 9, and they are quite consistent with the changes calculated from weight measurements.
Figure 28 shows micrographs of specimens from MSR-FCL-1 after run 4 (9600 hr total exposure to the fluoroborate mixture).
The left micrograph shows the surface of the specimen at the hottest position $(588^{\circ}C)$ .
Hastelloy N Specimen 4 from MSR-FCL-1 Exposed to $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole % NaF at $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 9600 hr (Through Run 4).
Average Thickness Change of Specimens After 9600 hr Total Exposure to $\mathsf{NaBF}_4$ -8 mole % NaF in MSR-FCL-1
Hastelloy N Specimens from MSR-FCL-1 Exposed to $\mathsf{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole $\%$ NaF for 9600 hr (4 Runs).
Average weight losses of the specimens are $21.3\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
Microprobe analysis disclosed a concentration gradient of chromium and iron for a distance of 0.6 mil.
There is less than 1.0 wt $\%$ Cr and only 1.0 wt $\%$ Fe 0.4 mil into the specimen.
This depleted area is represented by the darkened area extending from the surface for approximately 0.5 mil.
The center micrograph shows the surface of a specimen at $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The average weight loss of these specimens was $4.6\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
Microprobe analysis of these specimens disclosed only a small concentration gradient for a distance of 0.1 mil.
The micrograph to the right shows the deposit on the surface of the specimens at $510^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The average weight loss of the specimen is $0.1\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ , but this overall loss stems from a great deal of corrosion on the leading edge and is not indicative of the whole specimen.
This situation existed for a distance of 0.5 mil, after which the concentrations of both elements approached that of the matrix.
The average deposit is only 0.2 mil thick; thus, it appears that portions of the deposit have diffused in.
Run 5 was terminated after $422\mathrm{hr}$ of operation at design conditions when the pump bearings failed.
A total of $3.31\mathrm{g}$ of Hastelloy N particles was recovered from the loop.
A nickel filter with $40 - \mu \mathrm{m}$ pores was also installed in the dump tank line to filter any metal particles from the salt being returned to the loop.

With the enlarged access port we were able to insert a 5/8-in.-OD cold finger with circumferential grooves that had been previously used in another circulating fluoroborate loop (PKP-1) and on which significant deposits of salt containing $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{CrF}_6$ had been obtained.
Several tests were made during run 5 with this cold finger at temperatures between 150 and $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The cold finger was inserted below the liquid surface in the pump bowl for periods of 5 to 6 hr for each test.
The salt temperature in the pump bowl was approximately $516^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Small deposits with a light-green color (indication of corrosion product $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{CrF}_{6}$ ) were occasionally seen on the cold finger when it was withdrawn into the sight glass above the pump bowl.
The weight changes of Hastelloy N specimens in loop MSR-FCL-1 are compared in Table 7 for each of five test runs.
The corrosion rate decreased steadily over the first four runs and, at the conclusion of run 4, had dropped to $0.3\mathrm{mil/hr}$ at the point of maximum temperature $(588^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ .
However, the corrosion rate during run 5 was higher than in any of the preceding runs.
Although the increased rate of attack during run 5 is undoubtedly associated with operating difficulties encountered during the run, we have not pinpointed the specific causes.
Increased corrosion is normally accompanied by an increase in impurities in the salt; however, we did not see any significant impurity changes during run 5.
Figure 29 shows specimens 2 (555°C), 4 (588°C), and 8 (510°C) at the end of run 5.
Note the change in appearance of specimen 4 between run 4 (Fig.
The etched appearance of specimen 4 after
Hastelloy N Specimens from MSR-FCL-1 Exposed to $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole % NaF for 10,000 hr Total (Through Run 5).
From top to bottom the temperatures were 555, 588, and $510^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The sixth run was abruptly terminated after 1241 hr, when oil from a broken pump cooling oil line ignited on contact with the pump bowl and adjacent piping.
Figure 30 shows the pump bowl and pump with no apparent damage after the fire.
Standard Hastelloy N Pump from MSR-FCL-1.
Impeller exposed to $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole % NaF 1319 hr; baffles exposed 11,371 hr.
the main loop heaters are turned off, and sufficient heat is supplied to the loop to maintain the loop circuit above the salt liquidus temperature, $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

During the attempted melting, the main loop piping (1/2-in. $-\mathrm{OD} \times 0.042$ -in. -wall Hastelloy N) ruptured at a point near the U-bend in the loop and adjacent to one of the metallurgical specimen holders, as shown in Fig.
However, approximately $77\mathrm{g}$ salt was lost from the loop.

Before the rupture the salt in the bowl and cooling coil section of the main loop had been melted without difficulty, the main loop piping had reached temperatures varying between 371 and $482^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ around the loop circuit, and the drain line temperatures were above $426^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

In particular, the temperatures under the clamshell emergency heaters (where the thermal insulation was about 1 in. thick, compared to about 2 in. on the remainder of the tubing) were about $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ below temperatures in the more heavily insulated sections of tubing.
The tubing that surrounded the specimens was removed after run 6 (11,371 hr) and examined by both optical metallography and scanning electron microscopy.
Figure 33 shows the tubing exposed to salt at 588 and $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
33(a) the surface roughening (some salt is still in place) can be seen, and the attack at the grain boundaries is noted.
Figure 33(b) shows much less attack at $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figure 34 shows the tubing downstream of the cooler in the coldest position $(510^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ of the loop.
34(c) shows a side view of the nodular deposits.
After the oil fire that ended run 6 and the subsequent rupture of the tubing during the attempt to melt the salt from the loop, the corrosion specimens were removed, weighed, and examined.
The weight loss rates found for specimens exposed to the salt at 588 and $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were (assuming uniform losses) about 1.9 and 0.5 mils/year, respectively, for the 1240 hr of run 6.
The corrosion rate ratio for these specimens (several times greater loss at $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was fairly consistent with past findings.
However, in earlier runs, specimens at $510^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had gained weight.
For this time period specimens at this temperature level lost material at the rate of 1.9 mils/year, the same as for specimens at $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
After repairs we returned the loop to design conditions for the start of run 7, circulating the same $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole % NaF coolant salt used since the start of loop operation.

During run 7 while repairs were .under way on the loop high-temperature protective instrumentation, an operating error resulted in a portion of the loop tubing being heated above $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
31) and also about 12 in. downstream.
Part of the salt in the loop (about 2 liters) was discharged into the loop secondary containment.
At about $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the strength of Hastelloy N is very low, and the salt vapor pressure approaches 1000 psi.
At the time of failure the loop had accumulated 10,335 hr of operation at design conditions (101 hr during run 7).
Following the rupture of the loop tubing that ended run 7, the corrosion test specimens were again removed for weighing and metallurgical examination.
The specimens adjacent to the ruptured area (hottest section under normal conditions, $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) were badly warped and partially fused to the tube wall.
The specimens in the intermediate zone ( $555^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) appeared to be unharmed.
The average weight loss for these specimens was $1.3\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ in the 101 hr of run 7.
Expected loss for these specimens in 100 hr would be approximately $0.1\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .

Thus, if we attribute the balance of the corrosion to just the period of the temperature excursion (to about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), the loss rate was $1.2\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ in 2.5 min or 10,000 mils/year (10 in./year).
Although the specimens were adjacent, one specimen lost $0.3\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ and the other $3.0\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
In the method that gave favorable results, a mixture of He, $\mathbf{BF}_3$ , and HF (purified with fluorine) was passed through the fluoroborate at $454^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The gas mixture was passed for 15 hr into a Hastelloy N vessel containing the impure (about 2000 ppm $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$ ) fluoroborate salt (2.7 kg) at $480^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The exit gas entered a solution of 10 vol % pyridine in methyl alcohol.
At the completion of the purification process titration of this solution with Karl Fischer reagent to the dead-stop end point indicated that about 600 ppm $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$ was removed.
Chemical analysis disclosed that the oxygen content decreased from 1700 to 1400 ppm and that the $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$ content changed

In corrosion work, we have long recognized the need for analyses that would allow us to distinguish between water and (1) compounds that contain $\mathbf{H}^+$ ions, (2) compounds which contain $0^{2-}$ ions, and (3) HF (highly corrosive).

On the basis of our tritium injection experiment[27] and work in the Analytical Chemistry and Reactor Chemistry Divisions, we now feel that the results of the water determinations included many other constituents of the salts, including oxides.

Assuming that $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ (or HF) causes the mass transfer in sodium fluoroborate and on the basis that 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ is required to corrode 1 mole of metal we have calculated the amount of water required to cause known amounts of corrosion on several of our systems (Table 12).

For NCL-17, in the 1054-hr operation before steam addition, $41\mathrm{mg}$ $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ would have been required to cause the $137\mathrm{mg}$ metal removed, leaving 75 ppm oxide in the salt.
At the end of 10,000 hr after steam addition $23\mathrm{g}$ of metal had been removed.
This would have required $6.3\mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ and would have resulted in 13,800 ppm oxide in the salt.
The amount of water required to produce the corrosion seen in NCL-20 is small but has continued to increase with time.

As for the second possibility, the $\mathrm{Fe}^{\mathrm{n+}}$ concentration in the salt has decreased over 120 ppm, which would result in the removal of only $350\mathrm{mg}$ of chromium from the Hastelloy N.
This still leaves about $600\mathrm{mg}$ that we might attribute to water-caused corrosion.

The use of equations of this type is given in a paper by Evans, Koger, and DeVan.[28] Of importance is the fact that at the balance point no material is transported, at temperatures above the balance point material is transported away, and at temperatures below the balance point material is brought in.

At a certain critical velocity (possibly even that of the salt in natural circulation loops), the path for step 2 would become short and step 3 would control.
Step 4, like step 2, would be velocity dependent.

When a velocity effect is noted, as is thought to occur in MSR-FCL-1, then it can be assumed that enough impurities are controlling the corrosion reactions to cause solution rate control of the entire process.

The effect of impurity oxide content in fluoroborate salt on weight losses of the Hastelloy N alloys at 555 and $604^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is given in Fig.

The data show that titanium-modified alloys lose less weight than the standard Hastelloy N for exposure in fluoroborate salt containing 500 ppm $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ impurity at temperatures between 555 and $604^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, at the same temperatures the titanium-modified alloy exposed to fluoroborate salt containing 1500 ppm oxide shows a greater weight loss than either alloy exposed to the 500 ppm oxide salt.
W = \left(C _ {0} - C _ {s}\right) \sqrt {4 D t / \pi},
43 shows that both fluoroborate salts (500 and 1500 ppm oxide impurity) are more aggressive than the fuel salt to the Hastelloy N alloys.
Also, it appears that identical rates would be found for the type 304L stainless steel-fuel salt combination and the titanium-modified Hastelloy N-fluoroborate salt (500 ppm oxide impurity).
The weight losses of the specimens exposed to the fluoroborate salt and the value calculated for $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 4000 hr are compared in Table 15.
However, in the salt containing 500 ppm oxide impurity the titanium-modified alloy showed smaller weight losses than the standard alloy, again because of its lack of iron.
In the salt with 1500 ppm oxide impurity more HF was available for attack, and the weight losses were higher, since measurable amounts of nickel and molybdenum were removed from the alloy.

Thermal-convection loop NCL-14 has operated successfully for over two years and the pump loop MSR-FCL-1 nearly one year with the fluoroborate mixture.
Comparison of the rates experienced by NCL-13A, NCL-14, and MSR-FCL-1, all circulating the sodium fluoroborate mixture, indicates a velocity effect on the mass transfer (Table 16).
At some purity level, perhaps 200 to 400 ppm oxide as analyzed, solid-state diffusion of chromium in the Hastelloy N will likely control corrosion, as it does in the fuel salts.

Titanium-modified Hastelloy N with less chromium and iron shows a greater resistance to attack in a fluoroborate salt with 500 ppm oxide impurity than standard Hastelloy N.
The corrosion rate of titanium-modified Hastelloy N in fluoroborate salt with 1500 ppm oxide impurity is double that found in salt with 500 ppm oxide.
